title: Variables & Types
description: Master Java primitive types, String, type casting, null handling, and final variables for interview coding.

sections.title: Primitive Types: int, long, double, boolean
sections.explanation: Java has 8 primitive types. In interviews you'll mostly use `int` (32-bit integer), `long` (64-bit integer, suffix L), `double` (64-bit floating-point), and `boolean` (true/false). Declare with the type keyword, assign with `=`. Primitives cannot be null and have default values (0, 0.0, false) when used as class fields.

exercise.prompt: Declare an int named `age` set to 25, a long named `population` set to 8000000000L, a double named `rate` set to 0.075, and a boolean named `valid` set to false. Print each on its own line.

validation.message: Print each variable on its own line with the exact values specified.
Use the correct type for each variable: int, long, double, boolean.

exercise.hint: Declare each variable with its type keyword. Remember the L suffix on long literals.

sections.title: Strings and String Comparison
sections.explanation: In Java, `String` is a reference type (not a primitive). Create strings with double quotes. **Never compare strings with `==`** in interviews—use `.equals()` for content comparison. `==` checks reference identity, which can give wrong results. String concatenation uses `+` but is O(n) per concat; prefer StringBuilder in loops.

exercise.prompt: Create two String variables `s1` and `s2`, both with value "interview". Use `new String(...)` for both so they are different objects. Print the result of `s1 == s2` then `s1.equals(s2)` on separate lines.

validation.message: s1 == s2 should be false (different references), s1.equals(s2) should be true (same content).
Use new String() to create both strings and .equals() for comparison.

exercise.hint: Use `new String("interview")` for both s1 and s2 so they point to different objects.

sections.title: Type Casting
sections.explanation: Java does implicit widening (int → long → double) automatically. Narrowing (double → int) requires an explicit cast with `(type)`. Integer division truncates: `7 / 2` gives `3`. To get a double result, cast at least one operand: `(double) 7 / 2` gives `3.5`. Casting double to int truncates toward zero.

exercise.prompt: Given int variables `num = 10` and `den = 3`, print the integer division result, then the double division result (cast num to double). Then cast 7.89 to int and print it.

validation.message: First line: integer division (3). Second line: double division. Third line: truncated cast of 7.89.
Use explicit casts: (double) for widening, (int) for narrowing.

exercise.hint: Use (double) num / den for floating division. Use (int) 7.89 for truncation.

sections.title: Null Checks and Wrapper Types
sections.explanation: Primitives can't be null, but their wrapper classes (Integer, Long, Double, Boolean) can. In interviews, always null-check before calling methods on objects. Use `Integer.parseInt()` to convert strings to ints. Auto-boxing converts between primitives and wrappers automatically, but unboxing null throws NullPointerException.

exercise.prompt: Create an Integer variable `val` set to null. Print whether val is null. Then parse the string "256" into an int named `parsed` using Integer.parseInt(). Print parsed + 10.

validation.message: First line: true (val is null). Second line: 266 (256 + 10).
Use Integer wrapper type, null, and Integer.parseInt().

exercise.hint: Integer val = null; then val == null is true. Integer.parseInt("256") returns 256.

sections.title: Final Variables and Constants
sections.explanation: The `final` keyword makes a variable unmodifiable after assignment. Use it for constants and values that shouldn't change. Convention is UPPER_SNAKE_CASE for class-level constants (`static final`). In interviews, using `final` shows awareness of immutability and helps prevent bugs. Final references can't be reassigned, but the object they point to can still be mutated.

exercise.prompt: Declare a static final int `MAX_RETRIES` set to 3 and a static final String `BASE_URL` set to "https://api.example.com". In main, declare a final local int `timeout` set to 30. Print all three on separate lines.

validation.message: Print MAX_RETRIES (3), BASE_URL, and timeout (30) on separate lines.
Use static final for class constants and final for the local variable.

exercise.hint: Class-level constants use `static final`. Local constants just use `final`.
